sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content: Risk factors for ovarian cancer can be categorized by their type and associated attributable risk based on evidence strength. Genetic factors, such as inherited BRCA1/2 mutations, carry a high attributable risk (strong evidence). Reproductive factors include nulliparity (moderate to strong evidence of increased risk) and protective effects from lactation and oral contraceptive use (moderate to strong evidence). Hormonal factors like postmenopausal hormone therapy have a variable/moderate attributable risk. Iatrogenic factors (ovarian stimulation for IVF) and lifestyle factors (obesity, smoking) have weak to moderate attributable risk due to inconsistent data. The following cards detail each factor.

cards.detail: Carriers of confirmed BRCA1/2 pathogenic/likely pathogenic (P/LP) variants are at significantly increased risk. Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) is effective in reducing risk [6,7,8,9].
Not having been pregnant increases risk. Conversely, each pregnancy/birth confers a 30% to 60% decreased risk [10,11,12].
Breastfeeding is associated with a decreased risk of ovarian cancer [10,12].
Use of oral contraceptives is associated with a decreased risk of ovarian cancer [10,12].
Data suggest that postmenopausal hormone therapy may increase the risk, although results vary across studies [12,14,15,16].
The risk for ovarian borderline epithelial tumors may be increased after ovarian stimulation for in vitro fertilization [17,18].
Obesity has been shown to be associated with increased risk, but the data are inconsistent [12,13,19,20].
Smoking has been shown to be associated with increased risk of some histologic subtypes, but the data are inconsistent overall [12,13,21,22].
